# 📝 Notes

May 13, 2026

## 8am AI\!

Invited [David Olsson](mailto:david@atomic47.co) [eric@scroll.ca](mailto:eric@scroll.ca) [giroday@gmail.com](mailto:giroday@gmail.com) [hanif.janmohamed@gmail.com](mailto:hanif.janmohamed@gmail.com) [Fulvio Ciano](mailto:fulvio@atomic47.co) [scott@driftresourcetechnologies.com](mailto:scott@driftresourcetechnologies.com) [Jen Boger](mailto:jen@atomic47.co) [Shabraj Shahid](mailto:shabraj@mttr.ca) [spencer@ratiomachina.com](mailto:spencer@ratiomachina.com) [graham@ratiomachina.com](mailto:graham@ratiomachina.com) [Bailey Klinger](mailto:bwklinger@gmail.com) [andre.bertram3@gmail.com](mailto:andre.bertram3@gmail.com) [Spencer MacBeth](mailto:spencer@mttr.ca) [mike@smartdeskcrm.com](mailto:mike@smartdeskcrm.com) ~~[Jason Kryski](mailto:jason@mttr.ca)~~

Attachments [8am AI\!](https://calendar.google.com/calendar/event?eid=aW52N2w4MWJvNjJqYm5zYmQxZTMzYjllNmZfMjAyNjA1MTNUMTUwMDAwWiBkYXZpZEBhdG9taWM0Ny5jbw)

Meeting records [Transcript](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Dkd1NW84YI_ME3Q-Wa3xh7Hk0dX1s79NlwY-w7_1rF0/edit?usp=drive_web&tab=t.r0dukn82ol36) 

### Summary

Discussions explored learning philosophy and AI interaction via the development of Project State infrastructure for knowledge management.

**Learning and AI Philosophy**  
Participants examined the shift from transactional models to relational learning and defined conversation as fundamental data. They noted AI acts as a mirror, favoring Claude for oversight and OpenAI for technical tasks.

**Project State Infrastructure**  
The Project State system processes knowledge through transcription and classification to create durable intelligence. This infrastructure maintains a foundational corpus to support derivative projects while costing approximately 10 dollars monthly.

**State Management and Flow**  
Conversations shifted to the concept of mushin to address cognitive blocks and mental discipline. The primary decision was to frame mental states as state machines to improve work focus.

*Rate this Summary:* [Helpful](https://google.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_4YkxrBAaiTVqYCi?isGoogler=false&isHelpful=true) or [Not Helpful](https://google.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_4YkxrBAaiTVqYCi?isGoogler=false&isHelpful=false)

### Next steps

- [ ] \[David Olsson\] Contact James: Reach out to James to request a response and coordinate a chess match. Follow up if no communication is received to ensure progress on the intended collaboration.

- [ ] \[David Olsson\] Run Nutella: Execute the Nutella project software to test its functionality. Verify if the application can be successfully deployed and run in the current environment.

- [ ] \[David Olsson\] Activate Kanban: Set up and launch the Kanban board to track tasks and note taking activity. Ensure the system is functional and provides visibility into project progress.

- [ ] \[David Olsson\] Migrate Application: Convert the current application into a server based architecture. Improve performance and usability for the end users.

- [ ] \[David Olsson\] Discuss Strategy: Consult with Jen regarding the proper couching and positioning of the deck to improve audience reception.

- [ ] \[The group\] Try Exercises: Practice the series of Tai Chi moves discussed to help change mental state.

### Details

*Did the screenshots in this section make your notes [better](https://goo.gle/44IH7J9) or [worse](https://goo.gle/4b5ZWtJ)?*

* **Mindfulness and Teaching Philosophy**: David Olsson and Hanif Janmohamed discuss the difficulty of maintaining mindfulness while working in front of computers, leading David to replace the word "thoughtful" with "learner" in their project documentation ([00:01:13](#00:01:13)). They reflect on how traditional schooling mirrors a factory model, contrasting it with the innate, self-driven learning process that occurs naturally ([00:06:40](#00:06:40)).

* **Orchestration Versus Specialization**: David Olsson introduces the concept of "orchestration over specialization," arguing that specialization functions like the "deskilling" of a factory worker. They define work outputs as either functions or variables, suggesting that individuals often incorrectly equate their identity with the variable—the report produced—rather than the function ([00:07:59](#00:07:59)).

* **Relational Versus Transactional Models**: Hanif Janmohamed and David Olsson explore the shift from relational engagement to transactional contracts, particularly in school assignments and programming ([00:08:56](#00:08:56)). They discuss the "intelligence of the cell," referencing biological models and the idea that intelligence exists at the boundary layer between the environment and the entity ([00:09:48](#00:09:48)).

* **Conversations as Fundamental Data**: David Olsson expresses that conversation is the most fundamental activity in their work, which is why they prioritize transcripts. They mention a colleague, James, and express a desire to ensure their dialogue remains accessible for those unable to attend meetings in real-time ([00:11:20](#00:11:20)).

* **Learning Loops and Behavioral Change**: David Olsson shares how their work with project states has fundamentally changed their behavior, moving from session-based work to an appreciation for aggregate information ([00:12:09](#00:12:09)). This shift has resulted in the creation of 15 projects and landing pages, illustrating how new learning loops can drive productivity and behavioral change ([00:13:23](#00:13:23)).

* **Technical Exploration and AI Interaction**: Hanif Janmohamed recounts using ChatGPT for technical tasks, specifically related to stone drilling, but notes frustration with the AI's tendency toward "AI-plaining" ([00:14:41](#00:14:41)). They describe how directly asking the AI to remove this tone resulted in a clearer, more human-readable output, which both David and Hanif interpret as a form of "proto-feeling" on the part of the AI ([00:15:47](#00:15:47)) ([00:18:31](#00:18:31)).

* **Claude Versus OpenAI Utility**: David Olsson explains their strategy for selecting large language models, stating that they prefer using Claude as a product for knowledge workers while relying on OpenAI as a standalone large language model ([00:20:54](#00:20:54)). They note that while Claude is currently more pedantic, it allows for better oversight, whereas OpenAI is often easier to use for purely technical or business tasks ([00:20:06](#00:20:06)) ([00:21:52](#00:21:52)).

* **Reflective Mirroring in AI**: David Olsson and Hanif Janmohamed discuss the machine-user dynamic, noting that the AI acts as a mirror for the user ([00:25:07](#00:25:07)). They reflect on the practice of saying things like "That's great" to the AI, acknowledging it as a way for the user to maintain their own flow and positive reinforcement within a feedback loop ([00:22:53](#00:22:53)).

* **Project State Infrastructure**: David Olsson details "Project State," an infrastructure layer designed to make intelligence durable across projects and organizations ([00:25:57](#00:25:57)). The goal is to evolve the system so that users can simply drop files into a directory, allowing the system to map the schema of how they think ([00:27:07](#00:27:07)).

* **The Process of Knowledge Mapping**: David Olsson explains the "Project State" process, which involves intake, classification, transcription, and enrichment through cross-pollination of themes ([00:31:29](#00:31:29)). They note that by utilizing a Kanban board, they can track notes over a 120-day period, categorizing them into diagrams, frameworks, and text to surface underlying patterns ([00:30:13](#00:30:13)).

* **System Costs and Scaling**: David Olsson reports that the "Project State" system, while processing significant amounts of information, currently costs approximately $10 per month, or about 10 cents per page. They note that they are currently refining the system to reduce unnecessary analysis and costs while maintaining the integrity of the data corpus ([00:33:58](#00:33:58)) ([00:36:45](#00:36:45)).

* **Foundational Versus Derivative Material**: David Olsson emphasizes the importance of maintaining an "append-only" foundational corpus that does not get deleted, allowing users to build derivative products from this base ([00:38:44](#00:38:44)). They compare this to how organizations function, with departments sharing pieces of foundational information to create new value ([00:40:02](#00:40:02)).

* **Marketing Strategy and Project Decks**: David Olsson demonstrates a new deck for "Merge Intelligence," a tool for merger and acquisition workflows ([00:44:37](#00:44:37)). They explain that their marketing strategy involves gathering foundational intelligence first, rather than starting with a deck, and positioning the content to be consumable for specific, qualified audiences ([00:47:04](#00:47:04)).

  ![][image1]

* **Evolution of Business Presentations**: David Olsson and Hanif Janmohamed discuss the changing form of business decks, noting that the traditional 40-page, information-heavy PowerPoint is often ineffective ([00:49:11](#00:49:11)) ([00:51:45](#00:51:45)). David advocates for a storytelling pattern—comparing it to the consistency found in Mr. Beast's YouTube content—where decks function as web pages that guide a user through a programmatic conversion funnel ([00:50:04](#00:50:04)).

* **Remote Engagement and Target Marketing**: David Olsson shares their approach to marketing in a remote environment, which involves identifying and targeting specific online communities rather than broad, mass-market appeals. They note that while current social media environments can be noisy, finding targeted groups allows for more genuine engagement and effective communication ([00:54:38](#00:54:38)) ([00:56:16](#00:56:16)).

* **Closing Summary on Learning**: David Olsson concludes the meeting by reiterating that the core challenge in business today is teaching people—and oneself—how to learn. They emphasize that individuals need to develop the capacity to dispose themselves toward learning, rather than relying solely on the shape or format of provided content ([00:58:06](#00:58:06)).

* **Mushin and Cognitive Blocks**: David Olsson describes the Japanese concept of "mushin," or "no mind," which refers to a mental flow state used in martial arts to ensure thoughts do not interfere with actions. David Olsson identifies this as a potential strategy for addressing cognitive paralysis, a state where individuals are aware of what they need to do but face a mental block that keeps them from acting. Hanif Janmohamed expresses agreement with this perspective and emphasizes that reframing is a vital tool for enabling learning ([00:59:31](#00:59:31)).

* **Physical and Mental State Alignment**: David Olsson reflects on the concept of "no mind" in the context of large language models, characterizing it as an "ultimate mind". David Olsson shares that they and Karen discussed using Tai Chi moves as a way to physically disengage from task content to shift one's mental state. David Olsson suggests that engaging in preparatory activities, such as doing jumping jacks or watching specific videos, can help individuals reach a productive state of mind before starting work ([01:00:38](#01:00:38)).

* **State Machines and Mental Discipline**: David Olsson connects the programming concept of a "project state" to the idea of a "state of mind," noting that treating mental states as state machines provides a useful framework for discipline. Hanif Janmohamed affirms that the mushin concept is effectively a state of mind. David Olsson relays advice from a therapist to avoid being distracted by mental "chatter," which involves deciding not to act on every passing thought. Both participants agree that it is not necessary to enable every thought, concluding that focus is similar to being alone in an empty room ([01:01:45](#01:01:45)).

* **Meeting Conclusion**: David Olsson and Hanif Janmohamed conclude the session by agreeing to continue their discussion through their personal channel ([01:01:45](#01:01:45)).

*You should review Gemini's notes to make sure they're accurate. [Get tips and learn how Gemini takes notes](https://support.google.com/meet/answer/14754931)*
